lesson.title: Slices
lesson.summary: Dynamic arrays — store multiple values of the same type together.

sections.heading: What is a slice
sections.bodyHtml:
An array in Go has a fixed size declared up front. A slice is like an array but its length can grow.

Think of a slice as a window into a backing array. You can make it bigger or smaller as needed.

Declare a slice of integers:

var scores []int

Notice no size inside the brackets. That is the difference:

  • [5]int — array, exactly 5 elements
  • []int — slice, any length

A slice declared with var starts as nil (no backing array yet).

sections.heading: Creating slices
sections.bodyHtml:
Three common ways to create a slice:

1. Slice literal

numbers := []int{10, 20, 30}

2. make

numbers := make([]int, 3)  // length 3, all 0

3. Slice an existing slice or array

original := []int{10, 20, 30, 40, 50}
chunk := original[1:4]    // {20, 30, 40}

The slice syntax s[start:end] takes elements from start up to (but not including) end.

sections.heading: Appending and indexing
sections.bodyHtml:
Use append to add elements:

numbers := []int{1, 2, 3}
numbers = append(numbers, 4)   // {1, 2, 3, 4}
numbers = append(numbers, 5, 6) // {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}

Access elements by index, just like arrays:

fmt.Println(numbers[0])  // first element
fmt.Println(numbers[2])  // third element
n := len(numbers)        // number of elements

Remember: indices start at 0. The last element is at len(slice) - 1.

If you index outside the slice — for example numbers[10] on a 3-element slice — the program crashes with a runtime panic.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/slices/main.go.

Create a slice of your five favourite numbers using a literal. Then use append to add a sixth number. Print the length and every element.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between [3]int and []int?

options.label: [3]int is a slice; []int is an array
[3]int is an array with fixed size; []int is a slice that can grow
They are the same thing with different syntax
Changes the length of the original slice directly
Returns a new slice with the element(s) added
Inserts an element at a specific position

questions.prompt: What does len(s) give you for a slice s?

options.label: The maximum capacity before it needs to grow
The number of elements currently in the slice
The memory address of the slice

questions.prompt: What happens if you access index 10 of a slice that has 3 elements?

options.label: The program crashes with a runtime panic
The slice grows automatically and returns nil

exercises.title: Working with slices
exercises.instructions: Edit main.go to create a slice literal of five numbers, append a sixth, then print the length and all elements.
